subject_profile.summary: Base country-level assessment; default category weights prioritize rule of law, institutional integrity, and personal freedom.
stated_values: Rule of law and institutional integrity weighted most heavily
Personal freedom and security from arbitrary state action are foundational
Economic success is contingent on rule of law, not a substitute for it

flags: Generated 2026-05-28 under v2.0: multilateral + independent (IMF, World Bank, ECCB, Freedom House, RSF) + international and regional press.

A stable twin-island tourism democracy on the East Caribbean dollar (XCD, pegged 2.70/USD via the strongly-reserved ECCB union), reliant on a Citizenship-by-Investment 'golden passport' program now under EU/US scrutiny.

Standout features: a recovered tourism economy and falling debt (~68%), against Paris-Club/domestic arrears, CIP-revenue fragility (an EU visa-suspension risk), Barbuda's lingering Irma vulnerability, and some governance/press-pressure concerns.

economic.notes: A peg-anchored, low-inflation tourism economy with falling debt but persistent arrears and CIP-revenue fragility.

fiscal_state.notes: Debt down to ~68% of GDP (2025) from 101% (2020) with improving primary surpluses, but significant arrears and high gross financing needs are an active IMF red flag. (IMF 2026 Art IV)

monetary_independence.notes: An ECCB currency-union member; the XCD is pegged at 2.70/USD - no national policy, but a strong, well-reserved union. (ECCB)

reserve_currency_intl_monetary.notes: The XCD is not a reserve currency. (definitional)

inflation.notes: Low and anchored by the ECCB peg; union-wide price stability. (ECCB; IMF)

banking_stability.notes: Backstopped by the ECCB (97% reserve backing, above the 60% statutory floor); credit-union supervision gaps and historical de-risking are watch-items. (ECCB; IMF 2026)

trade_actions_capacity.notes: A tiny, highly import-dependent twin-island tourism economy with minimal trade leverage and CIP-revenue concentration. (IMF)

institutional.notes: A free democracy with governance/transparency and CIP-integrity concerns, and uncertain arrears data.

rule_of_law.notes: A free, functioning democracy (Freedom House 'Free', 83/100) but governance/transparency and CIP-integrity concerns temper it. (Freedom House; IMF)

statistical_integrity.notes: The IMF notes domestic arrears are 'uncertain in size', signaling material data-quality gaps. (IMF 2026 Art IV)

civil_service_capacity.notes: A small administration with arrears management and CIP oversight stretching capacity. (IMF)

press_freedom.notes: Pluralistic (OECS group ranked ~48/180 by RSF) but criminal-defamation laws and politician-filed libel suits create chilling pressure. (Freedom House; RSF)

political_social.notes: Stable under PM Browne, with some concentration-of-power criticism and Barbuda-recovery tensions.

political_stability.notes: Stable under PM Gaston Browne with regular elections and peaceful transfers, with some concentration-of-power criticism. (Freedom House 2025)

civil_liberties.notes: Largely respected (civil liberties 51/60), with media-pressure and defamation-law caveats. (Freedom House)

treatment_non_citizens.notes: Open to investors/migrants via CIP and regional labor flows, with limited independent reporting. (lower confidence)

social_cohesion.notes: Broadly cohesive, with Barbuda-Antigua tensions over land/recovery and CIP land issues a localized strain. (observed)

demographics.notes: A small (~94k) but stable population with a tourism-skilled workforce and modest growth. (World Bank)

geopolitical.notes: Western/CARICOM-aligned but balancing China, with CIP a channel of Western due-diligence friction.

alliance_reliability.notes: Western/Commonwealth/CARICOM-aligned but actively balancing US and China ties, with CIP creating Western due-diligence friction. (The Diplomat; State Dept)

conflict_involvement.notes: No conflicts or active disputes in a peaceful neighborhood. (observed)

sanctions_capital_controls.notes: Not itself sanctioned, but CIP exposes it to EU visa-suspension risk and Western scrutiny over investor-citizenship. (EU; Astons)

physical_practical.notes: Extreme hurricane exposure (Barbuda ~90% destroyed by Irma), with micro-state service limits.

climate.notes: Extreme hurricane exposure - Barbuda was ~90% destroyed by Irma (2017) - in a low-lying twin-island geography. (Freedom House; CCRIF context)

healthcare.notes: Limited tertiary capacity typical of a micro-state, reliant on regional/overseas referral. (general)

infrastructure.notes: Reasonable tourism infrastructure (airport, ports), but Barbuda recovery is incomplete and disaster-rebuild burdens recur. (observed)

crime_safety.notes: A lower violent-crime profile than the mainland Caribbean - tourism-oriented safety with petty-crime concerns. (lower confidence)

disaster_insurance.notes: A CCRIF parametric member, but with a large protection gap relative to catastrophic-storm exposure. (CCRIF)

summary: A stable twin-island democracy whose tourism-led economy has fully recovered past pre-pandemic levels (growth ~3% in 2025) and whose public debt has fallen to ~68% of GDP. Its core vulnerabilities are fiscal-financial: persistent Paris Club and domestic arrears (the latter of 'uncertain size'), elevated financing needs, and heavy reliance on a Citizenship-by-Investment program worth ~1% of GDP that now faces an EU regulation threatening visa-free Schengen access. Currency stability is borrowed from the robustly reserved ECCB union, and the standout physical risk is acute hurricane exposure exemplified by Barbuda's near-total destruction in Irma (2017). Net profile is neutral-to-slightly-positive, gated by data opacity and CIP-revenue fragility.
